article: Background: Cardiovascular disease remains one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide. Early detection of inflammatory and metabolic markers may improve clinical management and reduce long-term complications. This simulated biomedical study investigates the relationship between a personalized nutritional intervention and short-term cardiovascular outcomes in adults with metabolic syndrome. Metabolic syndrome is characterized by obesity, hypertension, insulin resistance, and dyslipidemia. Patients with metabolic syndrome are at increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes, myocardial infarction, stroke, and chronic kidney disease. Despite existing preventive strategies, many patients continue to experience disease progression due to poor adherence to lifestyle recommendations and limited access to personalized care. Recent advances in digital health monitoring and nutritional science suggest that individualized interventions may improve adherence and patient outcomes. Objective: The objective of this study was to evaluate whether a structured dietary program combined with remote patient monitoring could improve inflammatory biomarkers, body weight, and cardiovascular indicators over a twelve-week period. Methods: A prospective controlled study was conducted at a university-affiliated medical center between January and June 2025. A total of 120 adult participants between 35 and 70 years of age were enrolled. Patients were divided into two groups: an intervention group receiving a personalized nutritional program and a control group receiving standard dietary counseling. Inclusion criteria included obesity, elevated fasting glucose, hypertension, and elevated triglycerides. Exclusion criteria included pregnancy, severe renal impairment, active malignancy, recent cardiovascular surgery, or inability to participate in remote monitoring sessions. The intervention group received weekly telemedicine consultations with a registered dietitian, individualized meal plans, and digital wearable devices capable of recording daily activity levels and heart rate. The control group received a single baseline consultation and general educational materials regarding cardiovascular health. Baseline measurements included blood pressure, fasting glucose, C-reactive protein, body mass index, waist circumference, and lipid profile. Follow-up assessments were performed every four weeks. abstract: This simulated study evaluates a new biomedical treatment and its effect on patient outcomes. The results suggest potential short-term benefits, while emphasizing the need for further validation on a larger cohort
